subtopic_detail: Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state with marked sodium derangement
stem: A 78-year-old man with type 2 diabetes is brought to ED with 5 days of progressive lethargy and reduced oral intake during a urinary tract infection. He has been incrementally less responsive over 24 hours. He is on metformin and gliclazide; he ran out of insulin three weeks ago. He is dry on examination with a tongue furrow and reduced skin turgor. There is no Kussmaul breathing and no significant ketonuria.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64, HR 112, RR 20, SpO2 96% on room air, Temp 37.6 degrees Celsius, GCS 12
data_table.Examination: Markedly dehydrated mucous membranes, no air hunger, lung bases clear
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 52 mmol/L, Na 152 mmol/L, K 4.6 mmol/L, urea 22 mmol/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L, calculated osmolality 358 mOsm/kg, capillary ketones 0.5 mmol/L, pH 7.34, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, urinary nitrites positive

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Intravenous 0.9 percent sodium chloride at 15 mL/kg over 1 hour, then insulin at 0.05 units/kg/hour after fluid response, with potassium replacement and capped corrected sodium fall
options.rationale: HHS requires cautious volume resuscitation first; insulin starts after fluid response and at lower rates than DKA, with sodium fall capped at 4 to 8 mmol/L per 24 hours to avoid pontine myelinolysis or cerebral oedema. (Source: JBDS-IP guideline for HHS adapted for AU practice; ADIPS resources)

options.text: Intravenous 0.45 percent sodium chloride at 1 litre per hour for the first 4 hours with a concurrent insulin bolus of 0.1 units/kg, no potassium addition initially
options.rationale: Rapid hypotonic correction and early insulin bolus risk rapid osmolar shifts, cerebral oedema and hypokalaemia. (Source: JBDS-IP HHS guideline)

options.text: Intravenous insulin bolus 20 units stat followed by 0.1 units/kg/hour insulin infusion and DKA-style high-dose insulin therapy without prior fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: HHS is more dehydrated than acidotic; fluid first, low-dose insulin after fluid response, not DKA-style. (Source: JBDS-IP HHS guideline)

options.text: Hypertonic 3 percent saline 100 mL boluses with reassessment for cerebral oedema and ongoing infusion to correct the hyperosmolar state efficiently
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is not used in HHS; it would worsen the hyperosmolar state. (Source: JBDS-IP HHS guideline)

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol over 30 minutes as initial therapy to correct acidaemia and an empirical insulin infusion at 0.2 units/kg/hour
options.rationale: Acidaemia is mild and not driven by ketoacidosis; bicarbonate is not indicated and high-rate insulin risks rapid osmolar shift. (Source: JBDS-IP HHS guideline)

explanation.summary: HHS presents with marked hyperglycaemia, hyperosmolality and dehydration without significant ketoacidosis. Initial management is cautious volume resuscitation with isotonic crystalloid, gradual correction of osmolality (sodium fall 4 to 8 mmol/L per 24 hours), low-rate insulin (0.05 units/kg/hour) started after fluid response, and potassium replacement when below 5.0 with adequate urine output. Source control of any precipitant (such as UTI) is essential. Cerebral oedema and central pontine myelinolysis are the feared correction-rate complications.
key_points: HHS: fluid first with isotonic crystalloid, then low-rate insulin at 0.05 units/kg/hour
Limit corrected sodium fall to 4 to 8 mmol/L per 24 hours
Treat the precipitating infection or omission of insulin
explanation.pearls: Capping the fall in corrected sodium at 8 mmol/L per 24 hours and using insulin at 0.05 units/kg/hour after fluid response prevents the cerebral oedema and pontine myelinolysis that follow over-aggressive HHS correction.

sources.label: Australian Diabetes Society inpatient hyperglycaemia statement

subtopic_detail: Necrotising soft tissue infection in a person with diabetes
stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with rapidly worsening left thigh pain and swelling 36 hours after a minor abrasion. The pain is now out of proportion to visible skin findings. There is dusky discolouration with crepitus on palpation. He is febrile, tachycardic and hypotensive. He has had two episodes of vomiting and is becoming confused. Bedside blood gas shows lactate 5.8 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 88/52, HR 132, RR 26, SpO2 96% on room air, Temp 39.1 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Left thigh dusky violaceous discolouration, palpable crepitus, exquisite tenderness, anaesthesia in the centre of the lesion
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22 x10^9/L, CRP 318 mg/L, Na 132 mmol/L, glucose 18 mmol/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L, lactate 5.8 mmol/L; LRINEC score calculated at 9

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg six-hourly with discharge home for 24-hour reassessment by the community ambulatory care nurse-led wound clinic and a GP review the following morning
options.rationale: This is necrotising fasciitis with septic shock; oral therapy at home is unsafe. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections

options.text: Immediate resuscitation, intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam plus clindamycin plus vancomycin, blood cultures, urgent surgical consult for theatre-based debridement
options.rationale: Necrotising soft tissue infection requires emergent broad-spectrum antibiotics including a toxin-inhibitor (clindamycin) and MRSA cover, with surgical debridement within hours as the definitive treatment. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections

options.text: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy as the sole initial modality, with antibiotic therapy and surgical debridement deferred until completion of two sessions
options.rationale: Hyperbaric oxygen is an adjunct after antibiotics and debridement, not a substitute. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections

options.text: Topical iodine dressings and limb elevation with intravenous flucloxacillin monotherapy, with surgical review deferred until cultures and imaging confirm a clear diagnosis
options.rationale: Waiting for confirmatory imaging in a clinical necrotising soft tissue infection delays life-saving surgery; monotherapy under-covers the polymicrobial picture. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections

options.text: Single dose intravenous vancomycin then discharge home with oral antibiotics, with a community wound nurse review arranged for the following morning
options.rationale: Single-dose therapy and discharge are unsafe in septic shock from necrotising fasciitis; emergency surgery is required. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections

explanation.summary: Necrotising soft tissue infection is a surgical emergency. Pain out of proportion, crepitus, dusky skin, bullae and cutaneous anaesthesia raise suspicion; LRINEC above 6 supports it. Management is simultaneous resuscitation, blood cultures, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam plus clindamycin plus vancomycin or alternatives per eTG), and urgent surgical debridement within hours. Hyperbaric oxygen is an adjunct after debridement. Delay to theatre is the dominant driver of mortality.
key_points: LRINEC above 6 with clinical features mandates immediate surgical consult
Empiric IV piperacillin-tazobactam plus clindamycin plus vancomycin covers the polymicrobial picture
Surgical debridement is the definitive treatment and is the strongest predictor of survival
explanation.pearls: Debridement within 6 hours of presentation is the single biggest determinant of survival in necrotising soft tissue infection; clindamycin adds toxin suppression to broad-spectrum cover.

sources.label: eTG Antibiotic: necrotising soft tissue infections

subtopic_detail: Workup of primary aldosteronism in resistant hypertension with hypokalaemia
stem: A 47-year-old woman presents with hypertension refractory to three agents (ACE inhibitor, calcium channel blocker and thiazide) at maximal doses. She has incidentally been found to be hypokalaemic on three separate occasions. She has no family history of hypertension under age 40. She has no episodic features of catecholamine excess. She is not pregnant. A morning aldosterone-to-renin ratio is markedly elevated.

data_table.Examination: No abdominal bruit, no Cushingoid features, normal funduscopy
data_table.Investigations: K 3.2 mmol/L, Na 142 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L; aldosterone 720 pmol/L, direct renin concentration 5 mIU/L, ARR markedly elevated; 24-hour urinary metanephrines normal; HbA1c 5.4%

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in the workup?

options.text: Cease all antihypertensive agents for 6 weeks with no further investigations performed in the interim period for this patient and arrange a remote phone review
options.rationale: Stopping all antihypertensives without confirmatory testing delays diagnosis and is unsafe at this BP. (Source: Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline: case detection, diagnosis, and treatment of primary aldosteronism)

options.text: Proceed directly to laparoscopic adrenalectomy on the basis of the markedly elevated screening aldosterone-to-renin ratio alone without further testing
options.rationale: A screening ARR is not diagnostic; confirmatory suppression and subtyping with adrenal vein sampling are required before surgery. (Source: Endocrine Society guideline: primary aldosteronism)

options.text: Withdraw interfering agents per protocol, repeat the ARR, perform a confirmatory test such as saline suppression or fludrocortisone suppression, then adrenal CT and adrenal vein sampling
options.rationale: The Endocrine Society pathway after a positive screening ARR is medication washout, confirmatory suppression testing, then adrenal imaging and vein sampling to identify a unilateral surgical lesion. (Source: Endocrine Society guideline: primary aldosteronism)

options.text: Empirical lifelong spironolactone 25 mg daily without any confirmatory diagnostic testing for primary aldosteronism in this patient with resistant hypertension
options.rationale: Empirical mineralocorticoid blockade may treat the BP but misses unilateral disease amenable to surgical cure and forfeits the diagnosis. (Source: Endocrine Society guideline: primary aldosteronism)

options.text: Send 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines and plasma metanephrines as the next step to investigate this hypertension as an endocrine secondary cause
options.rationale: Metanephrines screen for phaeochromocytoma; here aldosteronism is suggested by the ARR and hypokalaemia. (Source: Endocrine Society guideline: primary aldosteronism)

explanation.summary: A positive aldosterone-to-renin ratio screens for primary aldosteronism but is not diagnostic. Confirmation requires withdrawal of interfering agents (mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists, betablockers, ACE inhibitors as feasible) and a suppression test (saline, fludrocortisone, oral sodium loading, or captopril). Adrenal CT identifies macroadenomas but is insufficient for lateralisation; adrenal vein sampling localises the source for surgical candidates. Unilateral disease is treated with laparoscopic adrenalectomy; bilateral hyperplasia with a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist.
key_points: Confirm primary aldosteronism with a suppression test before any treatment decisions
Adrenal vein sampling is required before adrenalectomy because imaging misses small functioning adenomas
Bilateral hyperplasia is treated medically with spironolactone or eplerenone
explanation.pearls: After a positive ARR, perform a saline suppression test (2 L of 0.9 percent saline over 4 hours): failure of aldosterone to fall below 140 pmol/L confirms autonomous secretion.

subtopic_detail: Pituitary apoplexy with ophthalmoplegia and adrenal insufficiency
stem: A 58-year-old man presents with sudden severe occipital headache, vomiting and binocular diplopia. He has no history of head trauma. Pupillary examination shows a dilated, poorly reactive left pupil and ptosis. He is hypotensive and lethargic. MRI brain shows a 22 mm pituitary macroadenoma with central haemorrhagic transformation. He is on no regular medications. There is no family history of intracranial bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 88/56, HR 108, Temp 36.4 degrees Celsius, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Left third nerve palsy with dilated pupil and ptosis, bitemporal visual field defect on confrontation
data_table.Investigations: Na 128 mmol/L, K 4.0 mmol/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L, cortisol (random) 64 nmol/L, TSH 0.4 mIU/L, free T4 8 pmol/L, prolactin 12 microgram/L; MRI confirms haemorrhagic macroadenoma

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous hydralazine to reduce BP and prevent rebleeding without any concurrent corticosteroid because the diagnosis remains uncertain in this acute setting
options.rationale: Antihypertensives in a hypotensive patient with acute hypopituitarism worsen hypoperfusion; immediate corticosteroid replacement is the priority. (Source: Society for Endocrinology emergency guidance: pituitary apoplexy)
source_refs: Society for Endocrinology pituitary apoplexy guideline

options.text: Conservative outpatient management with serial visual field testing in 4 weeks and repeat MRI in 3 months for this acute neuro-ophthalmic presentation in a stable patient
options.rationale: Acute apoplexy with neuro-ophthalmic compromise and adrenal insufficiency is an inpatient emergency, not an outpatient pathway. (Source: Society for Endocrinology pituitary apoplexy guideline)
source_refs: Society for Endocrinology pituitary apoplexy guideline

options.text: A single dose of intravenous dexamethasone 4 mg only for cerebral oedema, with no subsequent endocrine replacement and no neurosurgical referral arranged
options.rationale: Stress-dose hydrocortisone is required for adrenal insufficiency; dexamethasone alone misses the mineralocorticoid context and curbs further endocrine workup. (Source: Society for Endocrinology pituitary apoplexy guideline)
source_refs: Society for Endocrinology pituitary apoplexy guideline

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg immediately then 200 mg per 24 hours, fluid resuscitation, urgent neurosurgical and endocrine review for possible transsphenoidal decompression
options.rationale: Acute pituitary apoplexy with hypotension, hyponatraemia and a low random cortisol mandates immediate hydrocortisone replacement; visual field or cranial nerve compromise prompts urgent transsphenoidal decompression. (Source: Society for Endocrinology pituitary apoplexy guideline)
source_refs: Society for Endocrinology pituitary apoplexy guideline

options.text: Intravenous mannitol 1 g/kg as immediate osmotic therapy alone for the haemorrhagic mass with no corticosteroid replacement or surgical referral arranged at this point
options.rationale: Mannitol does not address adrenal insufficiency or cranial nerve compression; not the first step. (Source: Society for Endocrinology pituitary apoplexy guideline)
source_refs: Society for Endocrinology pituitary apoplexy guideline

explanation.summary: Pituitary apoplexy is haemorrhage or infarction of a pituitary adenoma presenting with thunderclap headache, visual or cranial nerve compromise and acute hypopituitarism. Hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia and a low random cortisol indicate secondary adrenal insufficiency. Immediate management is high-dose IV hydrocortisone, fluid resuscitation and urgent neurosurgical referral for transsphenoidal decompression when there is visual or cranial nerve compromise. Subsequent replacement covers thyroid, gonadal and posterior pituitary axes as required.
key_points: Acute apoplexy is a neurosurgical and endocrine emergency
IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus then 200 mg per 24 hours treats secondary adrenal insufficiency
Transsphenoidal decompression is indicated for visual or cranial nerve compromise
explanation.pearls: A low random cortisol below 100 nmol/L with hyponatraemia and hypoglycaemia in a patient with a pituitary mass mandates IV hydrocortisone 100 mg before any other intervention.

sources.label: Society for Endocrinology pituitary apoplexy guideline
Endocrine Society guideline: hormonal replacement in hypopituitarism

subtopic_detail: Early disease-modifying therapy in seropositive rheumatoid arthritis
stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with 4 months of symmetrical small joint pain and morning stiffness lasting 2 hours. She has bilateral MCP and PIP synovitis. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP are positive. Hand radiographs show early periarticular osteopenia without erosions. She is otherwise well, on no regular medications, not planning pregnancy in the next 18 months and has no liver or lung disease. DAS28 is calculated at 5.4.

data_table.Examination: Synovitis at 8 MCP and 6 PIP joints, two tender swollen wrists, no nodules; ESR 48 mm/hour
data_table.Investigations: RF 86 IU/mL, anti-CCP 320 U/mL, ANA negative, ALT 22 U/L, eGFR 88, hepatitis B and C serology negative, CXR clear, QFT-Plus negative

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial pharmacological plan?

options.text: Long-term oral prednisolone 10 mg daily as monotherapy with no addition of any conventional or biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic agent now or in the foreseeable future
options.rationale: Steroid monotherapy does not modify joint damage and incurs cumulative toxicity; a conventional synthetic DMARD is mandatory. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association rheumatoid arthritis position statement)

options.text: Naproxen 500 mg twice daily as monotherapy with no escalation plan and no addition of any conventional or biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic agent at any later stage
options.rationale: NSAIDs treat symptoms only; they do not prevent erosive progression in seropositive RA. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association RA statement)

options.text: Defer disease-modifying therapy for 12 months while observing for spontaneous remission and using analgesia as required during the wait-and-watch interval
options.rationale: Window-of-opportunity data favour DMARD start within 3 months of symptom onset; deferral worsens long-term outcomes. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association RA statement)

options.text: Hydroxychloroquine 5 mg/kg daily as monotherapy as the sole first-line disease-modifying agent in this seropositive, high-disease-activity rheumatoid arthritis presentation
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine monotherapy is inadequate for seropositive high-activity RA; it features in combination regimens but not as monotherapy here. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association RA statement)

options.text: Methotrexate 10 mg weekly with folic acid, a tapering bridging course of oral prednisolone, baseline screening completed, plus a treat-to-target plan with biologic step-up if no remission
options.rationale: Methotrexate is the AU anchor DMARD for seropositive RA, started after screening (CXR, hepatitis, eGFR, LFTs), with a short bridging glucocorticoid and a treat-to-target plan stepping to combination or biologic therapy if remission is not achieved. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association RA statement)

explanation.summary: Seropositive RA with high disease activity warrants early DMARD initiation within the window of opportunity. Methotrexate 10 to 15 mg weekly with folic acid is the AU first-line anchor drug, paired with bridging glucocorticoids and a treat-to-target plan. Screening before initiation includes LFTs, eGFR, hepatitis B and C, tuberculosis (QFT-Plus) and pneumococcal vaccination. Step-up to combination conventional synthetic DMARDs or biologic therapy follows failure to reach remission at 3 to 6 months.
key_points: Methotrexate is the AU anchor DMARD for seropositive RA
Early initiation within 3 months of symptom onset preserves joint function
Step-up to combination or biologic therapy when remission is not achieved at 3 to 6 months
explanation.pearls: Methotrexate started at 10 mg weekly with folic acid and titrated to 20 to 25 mg weekly over 6 to 8 weeks reaches remission in around 30 percent of seropositive RA as monotherapy.
